coredata.dc:description: TiO2 nanoparticles are promising photocatalysts due to their oxidizing strength and inertness. However, their inability to efficiently absorb visible light limits industrial applications that could use sunlight. The addition of carbon dots to TiO2 has been recently shown to have the potential to address this issue by enhancing the visible-light-driven photocatalytic efficiency of the resulting nanocomposites. However, concrete data on their sustainable development and fabrication is lacking. Herein, we performed a life cycle assessment (LCA) study to understand the environmental impacts of different TiO2‑carbon dots nanocomposites with enhanced visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity. It was found that the nanocomposites with the highest photocatalytic activity were the ones whose synthesis was associated with lower environmental impacts. Furthermore, the carbon dots generally have little to moderate contributions to most impact categories, except for marine eutrophication. Finally, the most critical parameter was found to be the source of TiO2 for the nanocomposites.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: TiO2 nanoparticles are promising photocatalysts due to their oxidizing strength and inertness. However, their inability to efficiently absorb visible light limits industrial applications that could use sunlight. The addition of carbon dots to TiO2 has been recently shown to have the potential to address this issue by enhancing the visible-light-driven photocatalytic efficiency of the resulting nanocomposites. However, concrete data on their sustainable development and fabrication is lacking. Herein, we performed a life cycle assessment (LCA) study to understand the environmental impacts of different TiO2‑carbon dots nanocomposites with enhanced visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity. It was found that the nanocomposites with the highest photocatalytic activity were the ones whose synthesis was associated with lower environmental impacts. Furthermore, the carbon dots generally have little to moderate contributions to most impact categories, except for marine eutrophication. Finally, the most critical parameter was found to be the source of TiO2 for the nanocomposites.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water scarcity is a global challenge that is aggravated due to an ever-increasing demand for drinking water. (Pimentel et al., 2004) Because of this, it is estimated that over 40 % of the global population will endure severe water scarcity within the next 50 years. (Mekonnen and Hoekstra, 2016) Thus, the protection of existing freshwater resources is of paramount importance for future sustainability. (Tollefson, 2011) Textile industries that employ organic dyes are responsible for the utilization of a huge amount of potable water, (Sen et al., 2019) as they require highly water-intensive processes that pose an environmental challenge. Although textile wastewater can undergo treatment for subsequent reuse, it may contain high amounts of persistent organic pollutants, inorganic salts and heavy metals. (Sen et al., 2019) Organic dyes can be hazardous to human health and cause dysfunction in several systems of human beings. (Adegoke and Bello, 2015) Additionally, these pollutants may originate damaging by-products via hydrolysis, oxidation or other reactions associated with an aqueous medium. (Konstantinou and Albanis, 2004).

Several strategies have been studied regarding the treatment of textile wastewater effluents. In general, conventional treatment plants have not effectively removed dyes from textile wastewater. (Konstantinou and Albanis, 2004) Some alternatives offering higher efficiency, such as chemical precipitation, nanofiltration, and electrocoagulation, have been reported. (Arellano-Cárdenas et al., 2013; Chowdhury et al., 2011) However, these technologies have a very high cost and high energy demands and may require toxic components to function as intended. Another option would be advanced oxidation processes (AOPs), such as photocatalysis. These processes have gained the scientific community's attention due to offering a higher degradation efficiency of organic pollutants while being relatively low cost and safe to use.

Photocatalytic processes are based on converting photoenergy into chemical energy, promoting a reaction without consuming the catalyst. It can be a greener alternative to conventional treatment methods and presents several advantages. Namely, it may employ an inexhaustible and widely available energy source (solar irradiation) and achieve virtually complete degradation of the target pollutant. (Daghrir et al., 2012) Out of the known photocatalysts, titanium dioxide is the most widely employed catalytic agent, as it offers a non-toxic and affordable catalysis that does not originate catalyst by-products and is corrosion resistant. (Sendão et al., 2023a; Sendão et al., 2022) Due to its nature, photocatalysis is a method that can present an highly efficient conversion of light into chemical energy. Thus, it is often the focus of environmental remediation and pollutant removal studies. (Chen et al., 2016; Das et al., 2019; Sendão et al., 2023a).

Despite being widely used, TiO2 also presents some limitations that must be addressed to permit a broader range of applications. Namely, TiO2 presents a poor solar light harvesting capacity, as it is only capable of utilizing UV irradiation due to its large bandgap and consequent narrow photocatalytic region (λ < 400 nm). This is troublesome given that solar irradiation is comprised mainly of visible and NIR irradiation, with UV accounting for only ≈ 6 % of the whole solar spectrum, and thus, the outdoor application of TiO2 using only clean and inexhaustible solar irradiation is prevented. (Barolet et al., 2016; Ren et al., 2007) Moreover, TiO2 also suffers from rapid recombination of photogenerated charge carriers, resulting in the dissipation of absorbed energy and poor photonic efficiency. (Daghrir et al., 2012) Finally, the semiconductor's surface presents poor affinity toward organic compounds such as dyes and drugs, limiting the reaction rate as the target pollutants do not reach the active sites easily. (Dong et al., 2015).

One of the strategies that has been gaining attention for addressing TiO2's limitations is to create nanocomposites between it and carbon dots (CDs). The latter are carbon-based nanoparticles, typically sized under 10 nm, that display several interesting properties, such as a broadband absorption range, high photoluminescence, water solubility and low toxicity. They are also inexpensive and easy to prepare from simple compounds. (Deng et al., 2021; Vale et al., 2021) As a result, they have been employed in applications covering several areas, such as sensing, (Simoes et al., 2020) photovoltaic devices (Zhang et al., 2013) and photocatalysis. (Saini et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2023).

In this specific area of TiO2-based photocatalysis, including CDs has been shown to enhance visible light absorption (the main component of sunlight) and the affinity toward organic pollutants by the resulting nanocomposite. (Gong et al., 2022) Given this, combining TiO2 with CDs is a promising approach to develop visible-light-driven photocatalysts that take better advantage of sunlight and show enhanced photocatalytic activity. In fact, several studies have shown that adding CDs to TiO2 leads to enhanced efficiency in applications as varied as hydrogen splitting, catalytic photoreduction of metals or the photodegradation of organic pollutants. (Aghamali et al., 2018; Ansón-Casaos et al., 2021; Choi et al., 2018; Gong et al., 2022; Sendão et al., 2023a; Wang et al., 2018).

Despite the overall success of TiO2 as a photocatalyst and considering applications like the photodegradation of organic pollutants, we must also consider the environmental impacts associated with preparing the photocatalyst itself. To achieve this, one can perform Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) studies to determine the environmental impacts associated with the life cycle of a product or process. (Finnveden et al., 2009) Thus far, several LCA studies have been conducted regarding consumer products containing nanomaterials. (Karan and Chakraborty, 2022; Li et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2024).

It should be highlighted that different studies concerning nanomaterials have identified their production as of environmental concern, with the required reagents and energy being relevant contributors toward the product's environmental impacts during its life cycle. (Garcia Gonzalez et al., 2022; Pourzahedi and Eckelman, 2015b; Tollefson, 2011) Given this, research efforts aimed at improving the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 must be coupled with studies identifying the environmental impacts of the preparation of novel materials so cleaner fabrication approaches can be identified and devised.

Despite this, only a fraction of studies developing TiO2-based nanoparticles include an evaluation of associated environmental impacts. (Caramazana-González et al., 2017; Fernandes et al., 2020; Grubb and Bakshi, 2011; Pesqueira et al., 2021; Pesqueira et al., 2024; Pini et al., 2015; Sendão et al., 2023a; Tsang et al., 2018) Knowledge about the environmental impacts of producing TiO2/CDs nanocomposites is even scarcer (Sendão et al., 2023a). Furthermore, this type of photocatalytic nanocomposites is still at an early stage of development, and so, there are still aspects of the overall process that were not focused on and require study and optimization. (Farjadfar et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2021) Namely, aspects such as solvent optimization, materials recovery, and waste management are major uncertainties that need to be addressed to reach a viable commercial development. The absence of information regarding such parameters consists of a limitation regarding LCA studies of this type of nanocomposites that must also be addressed in the future when the process is optimized. Thus, there is a need for a more in-depth understanding of the environmental impacts associated with this promising type of nanomaterials so that, ultimately, better-performing photocatalytic nanomaterials can be obtained with lower environmental impacts.

Herein, a “cradle-to-gate” LCA study was performed for the production of different TiO2/CDs nanocomposites using three different CDs and production pathways, termed NC1 to NC3. These NCs were chosen due to displaying a significant enhancement of visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity, between 210 and 367 % over bare TiO2, for the degradation of organic pollutants in water. (Sendão et al., 2023a; Wang et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019) Furthermore, these NCs were applied for the same pollutant, facilitating comparison between them, and the studies in which they were described provided all necessary data for performing an LCA study. NC1 is a nanocomposite previously prepared and reported by us, consisting of the conjugation of TiO2 nanoparticles made from TTIP and CDs prepared from citric acid and ethylenediamine. (Sendão et al., 2023a) NC2 was reported by Zhang et al. and consists of a TiO2/CDs/Fe2O3 nanocomposite. TiO2 was obtained from titanium tetrabutoxide (TTBO), whereas the CDs were made from citric acid and ethylenediamine. (Zhang et al., 2019) Finally, NC3, prepared by Wang et al., consisted of the one-pot synthesis of TiO2/CDs/Ag nanocomposites, using commercially available TiO2, silver nitrate and ethylene glycol as the carbon source. (Wang et al., 2018) Based on these nanocomposites, the present study aims to compare synthetic methodologies and identify the major impact hotspots associated with each synthesis.

This work is focused on the analysis of the environmental impact of three different nanocomposites published independently by different authors. (Sendão et al., 2023a; Wang et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019) To describe how each nanocomposite was synthesized, we provide the methods used by the authors, as reported by them in their respective works. These procedures were used as the basis from which we obtained the materials and energy consumed to produce the nanocomposites, as summarized in Fig. 1 .

Regarding the methods used by the authors, NC1 was prepared via a sol-gel methodology. (Sendão et al., 2023a) First, the CDs were prepared via hydrothermal treatment from citric acid (0.15 g) and ethylenediamine (166.9 μL) dissolved in NaOH 0.01 M (10 mL). The reaction mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined recipient in a stainless-steel outer shell and subjected to 200 °C for 24 h in an oven. After cooling to room temperature, the reaction products were centrifuged (6000 rpm, 30 min) and dialyzed (24 h, MWCO of 3.5 kDa) to remove possible by-products. Finally, the CDs were lyophilized, weighted and resuspended in deionized water to obtain a CDs solution of known concentration. Regarding the TiO2/CDs nanocomposites, for an expected yield of 0.5 g of NC1, TTIP (1.855 mL) was added dropwise to isopropanol (11.975 mL). The mixture was left stirring for 30 min, after which H2O (11.25 mL) was added dropwise, followed by another 2 h of magnetic stirring. At the same time, the quantity of CDs solution equivalent to 0.01 g of nanoparticles (2 %) was diluted in H2O (5 mL) and stirred at 70 °C for 90 min. The CDs were added to the TiO2 nanoparticles suspension, followed by another 2 h of magnetic stirring. The final product was washed, vacuum-filtered and dried overnight. Finally, the dried nanocomposite was powdered and annealed at 300 °C for 3 h. (Sendão et al., 2023a).

NC2, reported by Zhang et al., started with the production of TiO2 nanoparticles from TTBO and acetic acid. (Zhang et al., 2019) Briefly, 68 mL of TTBO was added to 50 mL of acetic acid, and the mixture was stirred for 30 min. Afterwards, it was transferred into a 200 mL Teflon-lined recipient and subjected to 100 °C for 12 h. The nanoparticles were then collected via centrifugation, washed, dried at 60 °C for 12 h and annealed in air at 450 °C for 2 h. By its turn, the CDs were hydrothermally-prepared (180 °C for 5 h) prepared from citric acid (6.3042 g) and ethylenediamine (2.01 mL) in deionized water (60 mL). After the reaction, the CDs were subjected to dialysis (3.5 kDa MWCO) and filtered. Finally, to prepare NC2, 2 g of previously prepared TiO2 nanoparticles, 0.68 g of FeCl3·6H2O and 0.4 g of urea were dissolved in a mixture of 30 mL of ethanol and 70 mL of water. 10 mL of CDs solution was added to the mixture, which was kept at 40 °C for 1 h. The mixture was then inserted into a Teflon-lined autoclave and subjected to 180 °C for 24 h. Afterwards, it was washed, filtered, dried and stored until necessary. (Zhang et al., 2019)

Finally, NC3, prepared by Wang et al., was prepared via one-pot synthesis. (Wang et al., 2018) Briefly, 1 g of commercially available P25 TiO2 nanoparticles was added to 25 mL of ethylene glycol. The mixture was stirred for 5 h until a homogeneous suspension was obtained. Then, 50 mg of silver nitrate were added to the mixture under stirring, after which the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and subjected to 200 °C for 5 h. The nanocomposite was collected via centrifugation, washed and dried overnight. (Wang et al., 2018) An overview of the inputs used to calculate the impacts associated with making 1 kg of NC1, NC2 and NC3 can be found on Table 1 :

This cradle-to-gate LCA study was performed using the SimaPro 9.5.0.0 software and aims to compare the relative environmental impacts of preparing the three TiO2/CDs-based nanocomposites considered in this study. This was done based on the reported synthesis process for the three target photocatalysts, considering the direct emissions and indirect impacts associated with the upstream resource extraction and energy generation. The production schemes for the nanocomposites were described in section 2.1.

To make the comparison between NCs meaningful, when considering the function-based FUs, the considered % ln (C) and k should result from the same type of photocatalyzed reaction. For this study, we considered the visible-light-driven photodegradation reaction of the organic dye Methylene Blue (MB) in water, for which all NCs were previously employed. (Sendão et al., 2023a; Wang et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019) MB was chosen as a model pollutant due to the availability of different nanocomposites prepared toward its photodegradation and the fact that it is a widely used textile dye (among other applications), often found in the wastewater of this industry, and linked with several health and environmental problems. (Adegoke and Bello, 2015; Arias Arias et al., 2020; Sen et al., 2019).

Data concerning the k for NC1 and NC2 was obtained directly from their respective papers, whereas to obtain the data concerning NC3, we used the WebPlotDigitizer tool (https://automeris.io/wpd/?v=4_8), version 4.8, accessed on August 8th, 2024, from Porto, Portugal) to extract and quantify the relevant data points. The % ln (C) was calculated via the comparison of the reaction rate constant of each nanocomposite when compared to simple TiO2, as shown below:

The environmental impacts were evaluated using foreground data from the synthesis procedures described in section 2.1, while LCI data for the background system consisted of average data found in the Ecoinvent 3.9.1 database for isopropanol (Isopropanol [RER] | market for isopropanol | Cut-off, S), deionized water (Water, deionized, from tap water, at user [Europe without Switzerland] | market for water, deionized, from tap water, at user, Cut-off, S), electricity (Electricity, medium voltage [PT] | market for electricity, medium voltage | Cut-off, S), urea (Urea [RER] | market for urea | Cut-off, S), ethanol (Ethanol, without water, in 99.7 % solution state, from ethylene [RER] market for ethanol, without water, in 99.7 % solution state, from ethylene | Cut-off, S), iron (III) chloride (Iron (III) chloride, in 40 % solution state [GLO] | market for iron (III) chloride, in 40 % solution state | Cut-off, S), commercial titanium dioxide nanoparticles (Titanium dioxide [RER] | market for titanium dioxide | Cut-off, S), ethylene glycol (Ethylene glycol [GLO] | market for ethylene glycol | Cut-off, S) and glucose (Glucose [GLO] | market for glucose | Cut-off, S).

The processes for CDs-NC1 and CDs-NC2 were modelled considering the methodology reported by their authors (Sendão et al., 2023b; Zhang et al., 2019) and described in Section 2.1 (background data on Table S1). The process concerning TTIP was made considering the methodology found in ref. (Falbe et al., 2007) (background data in Table S1). Regarding the process of preparing TiO2 nanoparticles from TTBO, the process was described in section 2.1, reported by (Zhang et al., 2019) and originated from the work of Jiang et al., (Jiang et al., 2008) with the method for the preparation of TTBO being obtained from (Caramazana-González et al., 2017) (background data in Table S1). Finally, the work of Pourzahedi and Eckelman (Pourzahedi and Eckelman, 2015a) provided us with the information necessary to create the process for silver nitrate (background data in Table S1).

For this study, whenever equipment was necessary and was of a common type across the studies, we considered that they had the same energy consumption. Namely, stages requiring heating were always considered to have a power requirement of 2500 W, independently of the nanocomposite. This was done to consider the fact that for the same task (e. g., heating), the teams could have used different kinds of ovens with varying consumptions of power, thus affecting the environmental impact due to the equipment itself and not because one process was more impactful than the other if the same equipment was used. Additionally, the power consumption considered for the study was proportional to the capacity of the equipment used. This allows us to take into account that the equipment was not used to its full capacity due to the processes being done at a laboratory-scale. That is, the capacity for these equipment is not scaled to the amounts typically generated at the laboratory-scale (they can have more capacity than what is really needed). Finally, it is worth noting that, in this study, some steps common across all synthesis processes (e.g. 12 h drying stage in an oven) were omitted, given that the respective inputs would be similar. Thus, the comparative results were not affected.

The LCA study was based on a cradle-to-gate approach, from raw material extraction to the fabrication of nanocomposites. Environmental impacts were modelled using the ReCiPe 2016 v1.1 LCIA endpoint method, Hierarchist Version, which evaluates the environmental impacts in three main categories of potential environmental impacts (endpoints): human health, ecosystems, and resources. Midpoint indicators were also evaluated: global warming (GW), stratospheric ozone depletion (SO), ionization radiation (IR), ozone formation – human health (OF), fine particulate matter formation (FPM), ozone formation – terrestrial ecosystems (OF-TE), terrestrial acidification (TA), freshwater eutrophication (FE), marine eutrophication (ME), terrestrial ecotoxicity (TET), freshwater ecotoxicity (FET), marine ecotoxicity (MET), human carcinogenic toxicity (HC), human non-carcinogenic toxicity (HNC), land use (LU), mineral resource scarcity (MR), fossil resource scarcity (FR), and water consumption (WC). Both midpoint and endpoint indicators were evaluated, as they provide different information on different aggregation levels. Namely, endpoint uses indicators at an higher level of impacts aggregation, while midpoint indicators are defined somewhere between the emission and the endpoint and are frequently based on a more robust modelling, thus providing more precise information regarding the respective impact categories. It is also worth noting that the Hierarchist model is commonly considered as the default model in scientific LCA studies (Chaurasiya and Singh, 2023; Diamanti et al., 2025) as it is based on the most common policy principles regarding time-frame and other issues, thus being the most suitable for our study.

Since the fabrication of photocatalytic TiO2/CDs nanocomposites is still in the research and development stage, assessing the environmental impacts of their fabrication is still uncertain. Thus, we performed a sensitivity analysis to help determine such uncertainties for scale-up production (Kazemi et al., 2018). This was made by considering “What-if” scenarios (Pianosi et al., 2016), achieved by varying the amount and type of materials used for the preparation of the CDs (± 30 % and using glucose as precursor instead, respectively), varying the electricity input (± 30 %) and altering the source of TiO2 of NC1 and NC2 to the commercially available P25 TiO2 used in NC3.

To obtain a comprehensive understanding of the relative environmental impacts associated with the preparation of the nanocomposites, we must not only evaluate them from a synthesis-yield viewpoint (e.g. based on the production of 1 kg of catalyst) but also consider that differently prepared nanocomposites will probably present different catalytic efficiencies under a similar set of conditions. As such, in addition to studies employing a weight-based FU, we also did studies using FUs based on two different indicators of catalytic performance. For one, we employed an % ln (C)-based FU, which takes into consideration how much a given CDs enhance the photocatalytic activity of the respective NC over bare TiO2. A k-based FU was also employed, which considers the different rate constants for the photodegradation of an organic pollutant (MB), under visible light irradiation, when catalyzed by the different NCs. Finally, a sensitivity analysis was conducted to evaluate the change in the environmental impacts under different sets of conditions posed by what-if scenarios.

We started our study by comparing the relative impacts of synthesizing 1 kg of photocatalytic NCs (weight-based FU). Herein, we aim to compare the contributions of each input in the process toward the different impact categories for each photocatalyst, thus identifying the environmental hotspots associated with each nanocomposite.

Analysis of the midpoint impact categories of NC1 (Fig. 2a, raw data available of Table S2) revealed that the highest contributors for most categories were TTIP and isopropanol, which are needed to prepare the TiO2 nanoparticles to which the CDs are added. This is not surprising, as TiO2 constitutes the vast majority of the material in NC1, with the CDs added in the synthesis stage as 2 % of mass equivalent to that of TiO2. TTIP and isopropanol are responsible for at least 60 % of the impacts associated with all categories except for marine eutrophication. TTIP is of particular importance for the categories regarding freshwater eutrophication, freshwater ecotoxicity, marine ecotoxicity, and mineral resource scarcity (accounting for over 50 % of the impacts). In contrast, isopropanol is the most preponderant contributor toward global warming, terrestrial ecotoxicity, and fossil resource scarcity categories. As for the CDs, even though they amounted to only 2 % of the nanocomposites' mass, they still presented moderate impacts for most categories, except when regarding marine eutrophication, of which they are the highest contributors. The impacts associated with electricity and deionized water are negligible, with the latter only appearing for the water consumption category.

Regarding NC2 (Fig. 2b, raw data on Table S3), the source of TiO2 (TTBO) is again responsible for at least 60 % of the resulting impacts for all categories except marine eutrophication, in which, again, the CDs are the most important input affecting the impacts. Impacts associated with CDs, ethanol, and electricity were found across all impact categories. CDs' biggest contribution was toward the marine eutrophication category, whereas ethanol and electricity's largest impacts were found in the categories of fossil resource scarcity and ionization radiation, respectively. The contributions of FeCl4, deionized water, and urea were virtually negligible for all impact categories.

Finally, for NC3 (Fig. 2c, raw data on Table S4), unlike NC1 and NC2, we found that the largest contributor was not the TiO2 source but ethylene glycol and AgNO3. The drastic decrease in the impact associated with TiO2 may result from the research team employing commercially available TiO2 nanoparticles rather than fabricating their own TiO2. Ethylene glycol, the precursor used for the CDs, was found to be the major contributor to the categories of global warming, ionization radiation, human carcinogenic toxicity, fossil resource scarcity, and water consumption, whereas AgNO3, used as the precursor for the silver dopant of NC3, affected freshwater eutrophication, freshwater ecotoxicity, marine ecotoxicity, human non-carcinogenic toxicity, land use, and mineral resource scarcity the most.

In summary, regarding midpoint impact categories for NC1 and NC2, the inputs associated with obtaining TiO2 were responsible for most of the impacts for all categories (except marine eutrophication) by far, which is expected given the high amount of TiO2 present in these nanocomposites. Similarly, in other reports concerning TiO2 and TiO2-composites, the source of TiO2 was also the main contributor to most categories. Namely, Pesqueira et al. found that ammonium hexafluorotitanate was the major environmental hotspot in the preparation of graphene oxide – TiO2 composites (Pesqueira et al., 2021), and Pini et al. found that a significant portion of the impacts associated with the preparation of TiO2 nanoparticles were a result of the organic precursors used in the synthesis. On the other hand, contrary to NC1 and NC2, the TiO2-derived impacts of NC3 were limited. Instead, most of NC3's impacts resulted from the precursors for the CDs and the silver dopant, which were used in large quantities and had demanding requirements for their synthesis. Zuin et al. found similar results regarding the preparation of TiO2 nanoparticles deposited on polyethersulfone membranes, for which the TiO2 was a minor contributor toward all impact categories and the polymer responsible for over 70 % of all impacts. (Zuin et al., 2013)

The relative environmental impacts of the three nanocomposites are compared in Fig. 3 , with the impact categories being divided into three main categories: human health, ecosystems, and resources. First and foremost, to produce the same mass of material, the relative environmental impact of NC3 is the largest for the human health and ecosystems categories and a very close second (96.5 % of those of NC1) in the resources category. This suggests that, overall, NC3 is the NC associated with the highest environmental impacts, likely due to using large quantities of ethylene glycol and silver nitrate, which are major inputs responsible for the impacts of NC3. On the other hand, the use of commercial TiO2 led only to a small percentage of the impacts being associated with the semiconductor, differing from NC1 and NC2, which can attribute well over 60 % of its impacts in all categories (except for marine eutrophication) to the source of TiO2. On the other hand, NC2 presented the lowest relative impacts for all three endpoint categories, indicating that its synthesis is associated with lower impacts (50.4 %, 62.3 %, and 66.1 % of those of the most impactful nanocomposite for the human health, ecosystems, and resources categories).

On a second stage, we again calculated the environmental impacts for endpoint categories of each nanocomposite. However, in this case, we considered a function-based FU based on the % ln (C) associated with the photodegradation of MB in water catalyzed by the nanocomposites. This FU was calculated by dividing the % ln (C) (calculated as shown in Eq. 1) of the best-performing nanocomposite by those of the alternatives, resulting in the FUs displayed in Table 2, which can be used to multiply the relative impacts of each photocatalyst based on how well they performed their intended function as measured by the % ln (C). This specific FU was chosen as it allows us to evaluate the enhancement of photocatalytic performance upon adding CDs to the TiO2 nanoparticles over bare TiO2 for each study. Thus, this section aims to assess if the enhancement in photocatalytic activity, originating from the addition of CDs, can offset environmental impacts that result from the synthesis stage.

The results revealed that, while NC3 is still the most impactful nanocomposite for the tested categories (Fig. 4 ), the scale of the discrepancy of its impacts when compared to NC1 and NC2 increased significantly. This is explained by the fact that, aside from having the most impactful synthesis based on the weight of produced nanocomposite, it also presented the lower % ln (C) (210 % versus 367 % and 366 % for NC1 and NC2, respectively). This resulted in a higher FU that furthers the difference in the relative impacts of each nanocomposite. Thus, it is no surprise that NC3 presents the highest endpoint environmental impacts out of the three nanocomposites. As for NC1 and NC2, because of their near-identical % ln (C) values, they present the same proportion of impacts observed with a weight-based FU, with NC2 presenting the lower relative impacts for all three endpoint categories closely followed by NC1. Once again, the impact associated with resources is considerably higher for NC1.

Finally, we calculated the environmental impacts by considering an FU based on the k of the photodegradation of MB in water when catalyzed by the different NCs. k can be considered an absolute measurement of the efficiency of the studied photocatalytic reaction. This k-based FU was also considered because while % ln (C) measures the enhancement in performance of the specific NC over the corresponding bare TiO2, it cannot show if the enhanced performance is significant when compared with other systems. That is, if the bare TiO2 considered in a study has very poor catalytic performance, even significant enhancements might not lead to an overall efficient catalytic activity. To better assess this, k is the most relevant parameter. Like before, the FU was calculated by dividing the highest observed k out of the three nanocomposites by the respective k of each composite (Table 3).

The study revealed an even larger discrepancy between the impacts of NC3 and those of NC1 and NC2 (Fig. 5 ), deepening the effect already observed when using a % ln (C)-FU. This is because the proportional difference between k of the three nanocomposites (0.00200 min−1 versus 0.00934 min−1 and 0.01105 min−1 for NC1 and NC2, respectively) was considerably larger than the difference between their % ln (C). This means that while NC3's % ln (C) - FU was 1.748, the FU when using the k as a reference was 5.525. This explains the larger difference found in the endpoint impacts of NC3 with this FU compared to the two previous studies. The relative impacts of NC1 and NC2 remain similar to what was previously observed, with NC1 presenting slightly higher relative impacts for the human health and ecosystems categories and considerably higher impacts in the resources category. This can be attributed to their respective k being somewhat close; thus, their respective FUs will be close and will not significantly alter the impact distribution.

A sensitivity analysis was performed with a focus on the functionality of the nanocomposites. Different scenarios were posed when considering k- and % ln (C)-based FUs. In each case, the scenarios included changing the input of the raw materials and electricity required for the making of the CDs by ±30 %, changing the identity of the carbon precursor used for the CDs (to glucose), and altering the source of TiO2 of NC1 and NC2 to that of NC3 (commercially available TiO2 nanoparticles).

Fig. 6a and b represent the results of the sensitivity evaluation regarding the variation of the inputs concerning the materials required for synthesizing the CDs. For both FUs, NC1 and NC2 display very little differences in impacts resulting from the 30 % variation. In fact, for NC1 and NC2 altogether, the biggest impact variation was found for NC1 in the human health category, varying less than 3 %, indicating that the synthesis of the respective CDs is irrelevant to the environmental impacts of the resulting nanocomposites. On the opposite hand, the resulting impacts of NC3 vary relevantly by altering the inputs by ±30 %, with the impacts associated with the human health, ecosystems and resources categories varying by ±11 %, ± 15 % and ± 20 %, respectively, for both FUs. This indicates that for NC3, the impact associated with the CDs can be significant, and small variations in this step will prompt relevant differences in the impacts.

As for electricity (Fig. 6c and d), altering its input did not significantly change the resulting environmental profile of any of the nanocomposites. This is not surprising when we consider that the contribution of electricity toward the midpoint impact categories was minimal for all three nanocomposites (Fig. 2).

Changing the CDs precursors to glucose (Fig. 6e and f) yields marginally smaller impacts, with decreases under 2 %, for NC1 and NC2, once again suggesting that the synthesis of the respective CDs is not of particular importance for the sustainability of these nanocomposites. As for NC3, substituting ethylene glycol for glucose led to a considerable decrease in the impacts in the categories of ecosystems and resources (22 % and 57 %, respectively).

Finally, the last “what-if” scenario tested the outcome of converting the source of TiO2 of NC1 and NC2 to the commercial TiO2 used in NC3 (Fig. 6g and h). Due to the nature of the sensitivity test, NC3 impacts did not change, as the TiO2 source remains the same. On the other hand, NC1 and NC2 both presented significant changes in their environmental impacts, with their environmental impacts decreasing drastically with the change of TiO2's source. Namely, the impacts of NC1 decreased by 75 %, 71 %, and 75 % for the human health, ecosystems, and resources categories, respectively, whereas the effects of NC2 decreased by 70 %, 65 %, and 62 %, respectively.

This reduction upon change of the TiO2 source can be explained by the large amount of TiO2 included in the composites and the much higher impact associated with the preparation of TiO2 from TTIP and TTBO compared to commercially available TiO2. In the employed version of Ecoinvent database, the input for commercially available TiO2 considers a mix of 50 % TiO2 prepared via the chloride method and 50 % via the sulfate method. By themselves, both methods result in significantly lower relative environmental impacts than preparing the semiconductor nanoparticles from TTIP or TTBO (Fig. S1). Preparation of TiO2 from TTIP proved to be the most impactful for the category of resources, while TTBO led to the highest impacts on human health and ecosystems. The impacts of the sulfate and chloride method were never more than 12 % of those associated with the most impactful method for each category.

In summary, the most important factor affecting the environmental profile of the nanocomposites is the source of TiO2. Thus, addressing this hotspot and correctly choosing the source of TiO2 could severely improve the nanocomposites' environmental sustainability. Furthermore, in the case of NC3, where the prominence of the impacts derived from the CDs was larger, altering the amount and identity of the materials used for the CDs led to relevant changes in environmental impacts. So, addressing this could be useful for this specific nanocomposite. However, it is worth noting that the catalytic efficiency of a nanocomposite depends on its structure, which in turn is affected by the identity and ratio of the materials employed in its preparation. Thus, while the sensitivity evaluation demonstrated that the source of TiO2 and the CDs are possible sensitive factors regarding the environmental impacts, further studies are recommended before varying the inputs and identity of the materials, as changing them will likely impact the catalytic efficiency of the resulting materials and may offset the expected environmental benefits.

This work provides a detailed LCA-based assessment of the environmental profiles of three different photocatalytic TiO2/CDs nanocomposites (NC1, NC2, and NC3) prepared for the visible-light-driven photodegradation of MB. The studied nanocomposites include different synthetic strategies, CDs, sources of TiO2, and catalytic efficiencies.

The analysis using a weight-based FU revealed that the nanocomposites with the highest photocatalytic activity were also those whose synthesis was associated with lower environmental impacts (NC2 and NC1). It was found that the main hotspot for the environmental impacts of those processes was the use of the chemical inputs needed to generate TiO2. Interestingly, for NC1 and NC2, the addition of CDs was found to have little to moderate contributions in most impact categories, except for the marine eutrophication category (in which the contribution was significant).

The preparation of NC3 was found to be associated with the highest environmental impacts despite including the TiO2 production process with the lowest impacts out of all 3 syntheses. However, the synthesis of NC3 included the addition of ethylene glycol and silver nitrate, which were the sources for CDs and Ag-doping, respectively. These inputs were found to have significant contributions to the environmental impacts of NC3, thereby helping to explain the worse environmental performance of its synthesis (compared to NC1 and NC2).

Different function-based FU were considered, but they did not lead to changes in conclusions over the analysis involving only a weight-based FU. A sensitivity analysis was also performed by considering several “what-if” scenarios. It was concluded that neither changes in electricity nor in the CDs led to significant changes in the relative impacts of the nanocomposites. On the contrary, the resulting impacts were quite sensitive to changes in the source of TiO2.

In summary, it was demonstrated that CDs can be used to enhance the performance of TiO2 without leading to significant environmental impacts during the synthesis step. Moreover, the generation of TiO2 itself was found to be the critical step in the preparation of the nanocomposites, and so, it should become a focus of future studies regarding the cleaner production of nanocomposites with enhanced photocatalytic performance.
